claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 10 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 19 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 2 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 10 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 2 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 8 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 10 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for.**

**How old is the child?**

Please let me know if they are:
- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children you'd like to claim for, please tell me each of their ages (and their names if you'd like me to keep track of them separately).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I've got three kids I'd like to ask about.

There's Alex, who's 10 years old, Blake who's 19, and little Charlie who's just 2.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hi! You're very welcome – I'm happy to help you work through the Child Benefit eligibility for all three of your children.

Let me note down their details:
- **Alex** – 10 years old (under 16)
- **Blake** – 19 years old (16-19 age group)
- **Charlie** – 2 years old (under 16)

Let me check the requirements for each child. Since Alex and Charlie are under 16, they have simpler requirements. Blake, being 19, will need a few more checks.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good progress! Now I have a few questions to determine eligibility for each child:

---

**For Alex (10) and Charlie (2):**

Since they're both under 16, the main requirements are straightforward. I just need to confirm:

1. Are Alex and Charlie **not** receiving any benefits in their own right (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?

---

**For Blake (19):**

Since Blake is in the 16-19 age group, I have a few additional questions:

1. Is Blake currently in **approved education or training** (such as A-levels, T-levels, NVQs up to level 3, or similar qualifications)?

2. Is Blake working **less than 24 hours per week** (or not working at all)?

3. Is Blake receiving any benefits in their own right (like ESA or Universal Credit)?

4. Is Blake doing an **apprenticeship**? If so, which type and in which country (England, Scotland, or Wales)?

---

Please let me know the answers to these questions and we can continue the assessment!

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Right, so for Alex and Charlie - no, they don't get any benefits themselves. They're just kids!

As for Blake, well, he's not in college or anything like that anymore. He finished school a while back. He's not working at the moment either - just at home with me trying to figure out what he wants to do next. He doesn't get any benefits in his own name, and he's not doing an apprenticeship or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for those details – that's really helpful!

**For Alex (10) and Charlie (2)** – great, they don't receive any benefits, so they're looking good so far.

**For Blake (19)** – I understand he's finished school, not currently working, not on any benefits, and not doing an apprenticeship. However, since Blake is 19 and **not** in approved education or training, I need to check something important.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. This is important for Blake's situation. Let me ask a key question:

**For Blake (19):**

You mentioned he "finished school a while back." I need to understand how long ago that was, because there's a possible **20-week extension period** for Child Benefit after leaving education – but this only applies to children aged **16-17**, not 19.

Since Blake is already 19 and not in approved education or training, I'm afraid the rules indicate he wouldn't qualify as an "eligible child" for Child Benefit purposes. For young people aged 16-19, they need to be in:
- Approved education (at least 12 hours per week of supervised study at A-level, NVQ level 3 or below, etc.), OR
- Approved training (not funded by an employer)

---

Now let me continue checking the remaining requirements for **Alex and Charlie**. I need to ask about your (the claimant's) situation:

**About you:**

1. **Where do you live?** Are you ordinarily resident in the UK (England, Scotland, Wales, or Northern Ireland)?

2. **What is your immigration status?** For example:
   - British or Irish citizen
   - Settled status (Indefinite Leave to Remain)
   - Pre-settled status
   - Other visa type

This will help me determine if you meet the residency requirements to claim Child Benefit for Alex and Charlie.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Oh, I see. Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester. I'm a British citizen, so no immigration issues or anything like that.

And just to be clear about the kids - Alex and Charlie both live with me at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Thank you for confirming that. Being a British citizen living in the UK (Manchester), and with Alex and Charlie living with you, you're meeting the key requirements so far.

Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment:

**About claiming Child Benefit:**

1. Is there anyone else who is currently claiming (or might claim) Child Benefit for Alex or Charlie? For example, another parent, a partner, or another family member?

This is important because only one person can receive Child Benefit for each child at a time.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming for them. It's just me - I'm the only one looking after them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, that's straightforward then. 

Now, one more check about Alex and Charlie's living situation:

**Are Alex and Charlie:**
- Living at home with you in a normal living arrangement, OR
- Currently in hospital, OR
- In local authority care, OR
- In a fostering arrangement?

It sounds like they're just living at home with you normally, but I just want to confirm this to complete the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Well, it's a bit complicated actually. 

Alex has been in a children's home for a couple of months now, but he still comes home and stays with me regularly.

And Charlie - she's actually been in hospital for a while. She's been there for about two months. I still visit her and buy her things she needs, like clothes and toys and her favorite snacks.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I appreciate you sharing those details – I understand things can be complicated, and I want to make sure we get this right for both Alex and Charlie.

Let me check the specific rules for each of their situations:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for explaining. Let me ask a few more specific questions about each child:

---

**For Alex (in a children's home):**

You mentioned he's been there for a couple of months (so more than 8 weeks) but comes home regularly. The rules say that Child Benefit can continue if the child spends **at least 24 hours a week at home**, OR stays home for **2 consecutive nights a week**, OR spends **7 or more consecutive days at home**.

Does Alex meet any of these? Specifically:
- Does he spend **24 hours or more per week** at home with you, OR
- Does he stay with you for **at least 2 consecutive nights every week**, OR
- Does he come home for **7 or more days in a row** at times?

---

**For Charlie (in hospital):**

You mentioned she's been in hospital for about two months (roughly 8 weeks). The rules allow Child Benefit to continue for up to **12 weeks** in hospital, and it sounds like Charlie is still within that timeframe.

You also mentioned you're still buying her clothes, toys, and snacks. That's helpful! To confirm:
- Has Charlie's hospital stay been **one continuous stay** (not broken up by coming home in between)?
- Are you **regularly spending money** on things for Charlie (like you mentioned – clothes, toys, snacks)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
For Alex - yes, he comes home every weekend and stays with me, so that's definitely two nights in a row every week.

For Charlie - yes, it's been one continuous stay in the hospital. She hasn't come home at all during this time. And yes, I'm still spending money on her - buying her clothes, toys, snacks, magazines, all the things she likes.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great news for both of them!

**For Alex:** Since he stays with you for 2 consecutive nights every weekend, he meets the exception rule even though he's been in care for more than 8 weeks. ✓

**For Charlie:** She's been in hospital for about 8 weeks (under the 12-week limit), and you're regularly spending money on her, which is also important. ✓

Just one final question for both Alex and Charlie:

**Is the local council paying for either child's accommodation or maintenance?**

- For Alex – is the council paying for his place in the children's home?
- For Charlie – is the council contributing to her hospital stay or maintenance?

(NHS hospital care itself doesn't count as council paying – I'm asking specifically about local council/social services paying for accommodation or upkeep.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
For Alex - no, the council isn't paying anything for him at the children's home.

For Charlie - well, she's just in an NHS hospital, so no, the council isn't paying for that either.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, that confirms everything I need to know!

**Great news for Alex and Charlie** – they both meet all the eligibility requirements for Child Benefit.

**Unfortunately for Blake**, because he's 19 and no longer in approved education or training, he doesn't qualify as an eligible child for Child Benefit purposes.

Let me provide you with the final outcome:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment – Summary

### ✅ Alex (age 10) – **ELIGIBLE**
Alex qualifies for Child Benefit. Even though he's been in a children's home for more than 8 weeks, he meets the exception because he stays with you for at least 2 consecutive nights every weekend. The council isn't paying for his accommodation, you're responsible for him, and no one else is claiming for him.

---

### ❌ Blake (age 19) – **NOT ELIGIBLE**
I'm sorry, but Blake doesn't qualify for Child Benefit. For young people aged 16-19, they must be in approved education or training (at least 12 hours per week of supervised study). Since Blake has finished school and isn't currently in education, training, or an approved apprenticeship, he doesn't meet the criteria. The 20-week extension for those who've left education only applies to 16-17 year olds.

---

### ✅ Charlie (age 2) – **ELIGIBLE**
Charlie qualifies for Child Benefit. Although she's been in hospital for about 8 weeks, this is within the 12-week limit. Importantly, because you're regularly spending money on her (clothes, toys, snacks, etc.), you would continue to be eligible even if her stay extends beyond 12 weeks. The council isn't paying for her care, and you're responsible for her.

---

## Next Steps

To claim Child Benefit for **Alex and Charlie**, you can:
1. **Apply online** at [gov.uk/child-benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit)
2. **Or by post** using form CH2, available on the GOV.UK website

You'll need:
- Your National Insurance number
- The children's birth certificates
- Your bank or building society details

---

**One thing to note:** If you (or a partner) earn over £60,000 per year, you may be subject to the High Income Child Benefit Charge. You can still claim, but some or all of the benefit may need to be repaid through your tax return. You can also choose to receive £0 payments but still get National Insurance credits.

---

I hope this helps! Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or the claims process?

reasonings: Alex (age 10) qualifies as an eligible child because he is under 16. Although he has been in a children's home for more than 8 weeks, he meets the exception rule because he stays with you for at least 2 consecutive nights every week (every weekend). The local council is not paying for his accommodation. You are a British citizen living in the UK, you are responsible for Alex, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

Blake (age 19) does not qualify as an eligible child. For children aged 16-19, they must be in approved education or training (at least 12 hours per week of supervised study at A-level, NVQ level 3 or below, or approved training not funded by an employer). Blake has finished school, is not in education or training, and is not doing an apprenticeship. The 20-week extension period for those who have left education only applies to children aged 16-17, not 19. Therefore, Blake does not meet the qualifying child criteria.

Charlie (age 2) qualifies as an eligible child because she is under 16. Although she has been in hospital continuously for about 8 weeks, this is under the 12-week limit. Additionally, you are regularly spending money on her (clothes, toys, snacks, magazines), which would allow eligibility to continue even beyond 12 weeks. The local council is not paying for her accommodation or maintenance. You are a British citizen living in the UK, you are responsible for Charlie, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for her.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is a British citizen living in Manchester, UK, and is the sole carer for all three children with no other person claiming Child Benefit for them. Two of the three children (Alex and Charlie) are eligible for Child Benefit. Alex qualifies despite being in a children's home because he comes home every weekend (2 consecutive nights per week). Charlie qualifies despite being in hospital because she has been there for under 12 weeks and her parent is regularly spending money on her. Blake, at 19 years old and not in approved education or training, does not meet the qualifying child criteria for Child Benefit.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Has the child (aged 16-17) left education within the last 20 weeks AND registered with a qualifying service?
response.help_text: 20-week extension available if child has left approved education/training and registered with: government careers service, Connexions, or armed services. Extension ends at week 20 or when child turns 18, whichever is sooner.

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child been in care for 8 weeks or less, OR do they spend at least 24 hours a week at home?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 8 weeks in care. Exception if child spends 24+ hours/week at home. Can claim again if child spends 2 consecutive nights/week or 7+ days in a row at home.

response.question: Has the child's cumulative time in hospital or residential accommodation exceeded 12 weeks?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 12 weeks in hospital/residential accommodation. CUMULATIVE RULE: If child returns within 28 days of leaving, time counts towards 12-week limit. Multiple stays within 28 days are added together.

criteria.calculation_method: Sum all hospital/residential stays. If gap between stays is less than 28 days, count both stays towards cumulative total. If gap is 28+ days, reset counter.
criteria.example: 10 weeks hospital + 15 day gap + 3 weeks hospital = 13 weeks cumulative (exceeds limit)

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.question: Are you regularly spending money on the child while they are in hospital/residential accommodation?
response.help_text: Exception to 12-week rule if you regularly spend money on child. For hospital abroad, only if they went abroad for treatment and you're back in UK. Partner's spending counts if married/civil partnered and living together.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

Although he has been in a children's home for more than 8 weeks, he meets the exception rule because he stays with you for at least 2 consecutive nights every week (every weekend). The local council is not paying for his accommodation. You are a British citizen living in the UK, you are responsible for Alex, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

Blake (age 19) does not qualify as an eligible child. For children aged 16-19, they must be in approved education or training (at least 12 hours per week of supervised study at A-level, NVQ level 3 or below, or approved training not funded by an employer). Blake has finished school, is not in education or training, and is not doing an apprenticeship. The 20-week extension period for those who have left education only applies to children aged 16-17, not 19. Therefore, Blake does not meet the qualifying child criteria.

Charlie (age 2) qualifies as an eligible child because she is under 16. Although she has been in hospital continuously for about 8 weeks, this is under the 12-week limit. Additionally, you are regularly spending money on her (clothes, toys, snacks, magazines), which would allow eligibility to continue even beyond 12 weeks. The local council is not paying for her accommodation or maintenance. You are a British citizen living in the UK, you are responsible for Charlie, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for her.

Two of the three children (Alex and Charlie) are eligible for Child Benefit. Alex qualifies despite being in a children's home because he comes home every weekend (2 consecutive nights per week). Charlie qualifies despite being in hospital because she has been there for under 12 weeks and her parent is regularly spending money on her. Blake, at 19 years old and not in approved education or training, does not meet the qualifying child criteria for Child Benefit.
